subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Two clearly drawn, labelled diagrams relevant to the policy discussion
Explanation shows how curves shift, how quantity moves from Q to Q*, and how welfare loss and tax revenue are affected
Diagrams are correctly titled and positioned to illustrate the externality and corrective tax
weaknesses: Minor technical mislabelling in Graph 1 (MSB vs. MSC divergence)
Omission of the MPB curve in Graph 1
Axis units could be more precisely labelled
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student made some technical errors (e.g. misrepresenting the externality curve divergence and omitting MPB), the two diagrams are relevant, fully labelled, and accompanied by a clear, accurate explanation of shifts, welfare loss and tax revenue. The overall quality of diagram inclusion and explanation warrants the top band.

strengths: Consistent and correct use of key economic terms (negative externality, MSC, MSB, MPB, welfare loss)
Appropriate use of terminology throughout to support analysis
weaknesses: Occasional vague phrase (âinefficient consumptionâ) that could be refined to allocative inefficiency
Notation consistency (e.g., Q* formatting) could be improved
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Economic terminology is used accurately and consistently throughout, satisfying the highest band despite minor lapses in precision and formatting.

strengths: Thorough application of the negative externality model and corrective tax theory from start to finish
Integrates price elasticity of demand when discussing policy impacts
Considers alternative measures and side effects in the analysis
weaknesses: Analysis remains qualitative without quantitative distributional or long-run dynamic exploration
Inconsistent reference to MPB in text vs. diagram
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The commentary applies relevant economic theory consistently and at depth, moving beyond basic description to discuss elasticity, tax incidence, revenue use and policy alternatives. This meets the descriptor for a thorough and well-developed application and analysis.

strengths: Clear identification of âEfficiencyâ as the key concept
Full and explicit linkage of efficiency to the climate tax, welfare loss reduction and social surplus maximisation
weaknesses: Definition of efficiency could be refined to emphasize allocative rather than technical efficiency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student not only names the correct economic key concept but sustains a clear, appropriate connection to the article throughout, meeting all top-band requirements.

strengths: Balanced judgments on the merits and drawbacks of the tax
Reasoning incorporates revenue allocation, elasticity effects and policy alternatives
Considers broader implications such as environmental benefits and tourism sector impacts
weaknesses: Evaluation could be further strengthened with empirical evidence or stakeholder-specific analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Judgments are effective, balanced and supported by clear reasoning, satisfying the highest band despite opportunities for deeper empirical or stakeholder-level evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: Analysis moves logically from identifying the externality to explaining how the tax changes incentives, raises revenue and may affect tourism given elasticity.  Alternative measures and potential side effects are mentioned.  Nonetheless, the analysis remains mostly qualitative and does not probe distributional effects or long-run dynamic responses, so it is appropriate but could be more developed.

Two diagrams are included and broadly match the policy under discussion; axes, curves and welfare-loss areas are labelled. However, key technical errors appear: the first diagram for a negative externality of consumption shows MSC diverging from MSB/MPB instead of MSB diverging from MPB, and MPC is omitted.  Such conceptual inaccuracy means the diagrams cannot be classed as fully accurate, so the strand fits the ârelevant but contain errors or inaccuraciesâ description.

Throughout the commentary the student links efficiency to the proposed Hawaii climate tax, explaining how the tax reduces welfare loss, moves quantity toward Q* and uses revenue for environmental restoration.  Connections are explicit, integrated and sustained, providing a full and clear explanation of how the key concept relates to the article.

The commentary applies the theory of negative externalities and corrective taxes from start to finish and briefly brings in elasticity of demand when considering possible impacts.  While the scope is confined largely to one microeconomic model, it is applied consistently to the article, meeting the descriptor of ârelevant economic theory applied throughoutâ, but the coverage lacks the breadth and depth expected for a âthoroughâ application.

Judgements are supported with reasoning about elasticity, revenue use, and possible economic impacts; however, reasoning remains general and occasionally speculative, with no empirical evidence or deeper cost-benefit analysis.  This places the work in the band where âeffective and balanced reasoning supports the judgmentsâ but stops short of the most robust, richly evidenced support expected for the top band.

The key concept âEfficiencyâ is explicitly stated in the header and repeatedly referenced in terms of achieving MSC = MSB and maximising social surplus.  Given that the article concerns a Pigouvian tax designed to correct a negative externality, efficiency is a highly appropriate concept; thus the identification is both clear and appropriate, meriting the top band.

The student explains what the curves represent, how the tax shifts the MSC curve, how quantity moves from Q to Q*, how welfare loss changes and how tax revenue is generated.  The explanation, while occasionally using imprecise wording, makes it clear why the diagrams are drawn and what they show, going beyond a mere caption.  Because some of the underlying analysis is shaky, the explanation is not âfull and accurateâ, but it is more than minimal, placing it in the limited-to-adequate band.

Economic terms such as negative externality, MSC, MSB, MPB, social surplus, price elasticity of demand, welfare loss and internalising external costs are used consistently and correctly in context.  Occasional vague phrases (âinefficient consumptionâ) do not detract from generally appropriate usage throughout the commentary, satisfying the highest band for this strand.

The student makes judgements on the desirability of the tax, weighing environmental gains against potential losses to the tourism sector, and suggests monitoring and alternative policies.  These judgements are balanced but not deeply nuanced (e.g. no stakeholder-specific discussion or quantitative weighing), so they fit the âeffective and balanced judgments are madeâ descriptor without reaching the sophistication implied by the very top band.

annotations.comment: Ensure inâtext citations and bibliography follow IB formatting; the commentary currently lacks full reference details.
The inclusion of Graph 1 on negative externalities is relevant and shows an attempt to illustrate market failure and welfare loss.
The student clearly identifies âEfficiencyâ as the key concept and explicitly links it to the analysis of optimal social surplus in tourism policy.
The definition of efficiency as a âratio of useful output to total inputâ is more engineeringâoriented than economic. Refine to allocative efficiency, where MSC = MSB maximizes social welfare.
The introductory paragraph explains the policy and links it to efficiency, but the economic definition of efficiency should be clarified as allocative rather than technical.
The diagram misrepresents a negative consumption externality â MSC should remain unchanged, while MSB should lie below MPB. Revise the curves to reflect MSB < MPB.
The diagram omits the Marginal Private Benefit curve, which is essential to show the divergence from social benefit. Include MPB in Graph 1.
The label âPrice of resources for protection andâ is truncated and unclear. Proofread chart annotations for completeness.
The narrative references MPB in text yet the corresponding curve is absent. This inconsistency weakens the analysis.
The caption âQuantity of Environmental Damage in Hawaiiâ is ambiguous. Label the horizontal axis as âQuantity of Tourism (visitors)â with appropriate units.
The description of external costs and market failure is clear, showing good application of theory to the tourism context.
Ensure consistent notation for the social optimum; use LaTeX formatting such as $$Q^*$$ throughout for clarity and precision.
Linking revenue of $68 million to environmental projects is effective, showing clear policy implications.
Consider distributional impacts on residents versus tourists; exploring stakeholder effects would deepen the evaluation.
Graph 2 is clearly titled and appropriately positioned to show the effect of the climate tax.
In Graph 2 the tax should shift the private marginal cost (MPC) curve, not MSC. Reâlabel the supply shift to MPC + tax to reflect internalization of externality.
The discussion of price elasticity demonstrates strong application of demand theory to policy analysis.
Reference empirical studies on tourist levies to support judgments; IB evaluation rubrics reward evidenceâbased analysis.
The exploration of alternative taxes and permits shows thoughtful policy evaluation.
Incorporate quantitative data or case studies (e.g., New Zealandâs tourism levy) to strengthen policy comparisons.
The conclusion reiterates earlier points at length; a concise summary of key judgements would sharpen the ending.
Discuss potential longârun dynamic or unintended effects (e.g., shifts in visitor demographics) to enhance the evaluation.
The phrase âinefficient consumptionâ is vague; use âallocative inefficiencyâ to demonstrate precise economic terminology.
Consistent use of MSC, MSB and MPB abbreviations shows strong command of economic terminology.
The repeated header âCommentary 1â on pages 1 and 2 is redundant; remove extra headers for better flow.
Axes in Graph 1 should include unit labels (e.g., $\$ per tourist, number of visitors) for greater precision.
